content.problemStatement:
You are given positive integers NNN and DDD. You may perform operations of the following two types:


add DDD to NNN, i.e. change NNN to N+DN+DN+D
change NNN to digitsum(N)\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(N)digitsum(N)
Here, digitsum(x)\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(x)digitsum(x) is the sum of decimal digits of xxx. For example, digitsum(123)=1+2+3=6\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(123)=1+2+3=6digitsum(123)=1+2+3=6, digitsum(100)=1+0+0=1\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(100)=1+0+0=1digitsum(100)=1+0+0=1, digitsum(365)=3+6+5=14\mathop{\mathrm{digitsum}}(365)=3+6+5=14digitsum(365)=3+6+5=14.

You may perform any number of operations (including zero) in any order. Please find the minimum obtainable value of NNN and the minimum number of operations required to obtain this value.
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and DDD.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing two space-separated integers — the minimum value of NNN and the minimum required number of operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The value N=1N=1N=1 can be achieved by 8 successive "add" operations (changing NNN to 101010) and one "digit-sum" operation.
Example case 2: You can prove that you cannot obtain N=1N=1N=1 and N=2N=2N=2, and you can obtain N=3N=3N=3.
The value N=3N=3N=3 can be achieved by one "add" and one "digitsum" operation, changing 999 to 121212 and 121212 to 333.
Example case 3: N=1N=1N=1 can be achieved by operations "add", "add", "digitsum", "digitsum": 11→24→37→10→111 \rightarrow 24 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 10 \rightarrow 111→24→37→10→1.
